topics.label: Offline Markdown editor
topics.summary: Markdown editing that stays usable without depending on a live hosted authoring backend.
topics.whyItMatters: Offline editing helps authors keep writing, previewing, and organizing documents even when a network path is unavailable.
topics.workflowLine: Teams use offline Markdown editing for drafts, docs, release notes, note-taking, and review workflows that should not stop when connectivity changes.

topics.label: Local-first Markdown workspace
topics.summary: A Markdown workspace that keeps normal writing, preview, and organization local by default.
topics.whyItMatters: Local-first behavior clarifies what stays on the device and what moves through sync, repository, or publishing workflows.
topics.workflowLine: A local-first workspace matters when teams want Markdown to remain durable and usable before networked services enter the flow.

topics.label: Markdown editor
topics.summary: A writing tool for creating and editing Markdown while keeping the source in plain text.
topics.whyItMatters: Markdown editors help authors move between readable source text and rendered output without hiding the underlying format.
topics.workflowLine: People evaluate Markdown editors by preview quality, portability, local storage behavior, file organization, and extension surfaces.

topics.label: Markdown preview
topics.summary: Rendered output that helps authors validate Markdown structure while keeping the source visible.
topics.whyItMatters: Preview reduces formatting surprises and makes it easier to inspect headings, lists, links, tables, and code blocks while writing.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown preview becomes important when teams need confidence that plain-text authoring will render correctly across docs, blogs, and app surfaces.

topics.label: Markdown documentation
topics.summary: Documentation workflows built around portable Markdown source files.
topics.whyItMatters: Markdown documentation keeps content readable in source form and easier to move through Git, publishing, and review pipelines.
topics.workflowLine: Teams use Markdown documentation for developer docs, product guides, internal notes, and public knowledge-base content.

topics.label: Markdown README
topics.summary: README writing that uses Markdown for project introductions, setup steps, and reference material.
topics.whyItMatters: README files are often the first durable documentation surface readers see in a repository or package.
topics.workflowLine: A strong Markdown README balances portability, scanability, setup clarity, and enough structure for both humans and crawlers.

topics.label: Markdown notes
topics.summary: Plain-text note-taking workflows built around Markdown structure and portability.
topics.whyItMatters: Markdown notes help people keep information readable, linkable, and portable across tools and devices.
topics.workflowLine: Teams use Markdown notes for meeting notes, design notes, release notes, learning logs, and lightweight knowledge capture.

topics.label: Markdown blogging
topics.summary: Writing and publishing blog content from Markdown source files.
topics.whyItMatters: Markdown blogging keeps the authoring source small, portable, and easier to move through publishing pipelines.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown blogging matters when writers want simple formatting, code blocks, reusable content structures, and easier versioning.

topics.label: Markdown knowledge base
topics.summary: Knowledge-base content managed through Markdown documents and structured navigation.
topics.whyItMatters: Markdown knowledge bases can stay portable while still supporting searchable, linked, and versioned documentation.
topics.workflowLine: Teams use Markdown knowledge bases when they need durable docs that can move between site generators, repos, and internal tools.

topics.label: Markdown project docs
topics.summary: Project documentation organized through Markdown pages, README files, and linked reference content.
topics.whyItMatters: Project docs need plain-text durability, easy review, version control, and predictable rendering across teams.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown project docs help teams keep setup, architecture, release, and troubleshooting information close to the code.

topics.label: Markdown writing workflow
topics.summary: A repeatable writing flow built around Markdown source, preview, review, and publishing steps.
topics.whyItMatters: A clear Markdown workflow helps authors move from draft to published output without losing source portability.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown writing workflows usually combine authoring, preview, revision, file organization, and output-specific publishing checks.

topics.label: Markdown for teams
topics.summary: Team documentation and writing workflows that use Markdown as the common source format.
topics.whyItMatters: Teams need content that stays readable in source form while still working in review, publishing, and collaboration systems.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown for teams is often evaluated through file ownership, review processes, portability, preview fidelity, and publishing confidence.

topics.label: Markdown for developers
topics.summary: Markdown workflows shaped around code, docs, review, and repository collaboration.
topics.whyItMatters: Developers use Markdown because it stays close to code, version control, and plain-text review workflows.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown for developers typically connects README files, architecture docs, issue notes, release notes, and rendered package or site output.

topics.label: Markdown theme packs
topics.summary: Theme surfaces that style Markdown authoring and preview experiences through governed contracts.
topics.whyItMatters: Theme packs separate visual identity from content structure and rendering behavior.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown theme-pack work matters when teams need consistent styling across editor, preview, and public lander surfaces without patching internals.

topics.label: Markdown extension workflows
topics.summary: Extension-oriented workflows that add commands, panes, integrations, or automation around Markdown.
topics.whyItMatters: Extension workflows matter when teams want customization without turning every Markdown behavior into app-local code.
topics.workflowLine: Markdown extension work is easier to scale when commands, settings, and runtime behavior are documented and reusable.
